speaker: interviewer
text: Zipora Nutavits-- now, you were saying during the break that the reason why and the other children were taken from your mother and from other people when the war was over and you were free-- could you tell me that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to tell you again. They don't take me from my mother. Nobody take me more. We was free. We can do whatever we want. But because I was very sick, they take me to the hospital. And I need to be in the hospital a few-- I don't know exactly the time, because I was very sick. I was very skinny, and I was very afraid. And I-- I was-- I feel again lonely in the hospital. I was crying again, but my mother coming to see me. But I want to tell you what happened in Budapest. They bombed Budapest. The transportation was very bad. Not the bus-- it was a lot of bus. So after the war, it was a big [HUNGARIAN] So my mother come to see me, but it was take times until she came to me. And after the hospital, they take me to other places.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is our big, big town, like the Los Angeles. I am here, and you are in Santa Ana. So you come to see me every time? I want to tell you they're different. So I don't visit my mother, I lose my mother. I love her, and she know. And she know when I lo-- when I left her that I'm going with-- with children to Israel. And she promised me, she says she come with me, with all my chil-- she came after me. So when did you get to--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I came early, in '46. And they came together me in '48 to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother and all your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I show you the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- Where did you go in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, this, so-- It was-- from Budapest we moved. I told you in Vienna, [NON-ENGLISH,] I was a little in-- in German. In German, we was-- we was learning a little. They tried to give us. And I told you they tried to teach us Yiddish. It was very, very di-- difficult. I understand everything, but not to read. And later, we-- again, they decide that if I want to go to Israel, we can't go very fast. So we they take us a very special-- again, They moved up, moved the people. They moved us to take us through to Ansbach or Ansbach. I don't know, this is a little place in Deutschland, in German. And it was a palace, a palace. A very nice place. And they collected always a lot of children. I don't know, about 500. And they, we was over there I think a month. I don't know. And we was over there very happy. We have over there a lot of chi-- a lot of teachers from Hungaria, from Polania, and from Romania. And they divide us from Hungarian, from-- because we don't know the language. You know, I understand Yiddish, but the children, it was a mix.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we have a Hungarian teacher, about three. And they collect us, and it was very nice over there. They make us couple of shabbat, and they light the candles and start to give us very good food. And they take care of us, and it was very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, I think about three weeks-- I don't know, don't remember-- my sister came to see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hannah. And I was crying, very but-- because they told that we are moving. And we get certificate, and we came to Israel. [NON-ENGLISH] In '46 we came to Israel. And in Israel we came to-- when we finish-- they take us, they take us to French. It was in Marseilles. I'll show you the picture. Over there, they collect a lot of children again. It was over the barracks. And we was over there a long-- again, a good time. They gave us food and very nice clothes. And again they ask if we want to go Israel. And I told, yes, I only wanted to go to Israel. Not England, not French, because they told we can't stay over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we was over there I think a week, and we take to a ship, and take us to Israel. I was feeling very bad about that. I was vomiting all the time. I was-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I was feeling very bad in the ship. I was about-- I don't know. I think it was 10 days until-- I don't know how long. You were seasick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was seasick. And then we came to Israel. First we came to-- to Egypt. We was over there one days. And I remember that the Arab sent us bananas. You know bananas? The first time that I know what is a banana? And I-- I ask, what is banana? And I thought, this is something not good food. So the teacher told me you can eat. This is very good food. So this is the first time that I taste--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Egypt. In

speaker: interviewee
text: Egypt. We was over there one day. And later we came to Israel. We came to Haifa. And we-- the English people was over there. They take us to Atlit. Atlit, this is a-- Atlit is a place that the people was over there. It was a clo-- this was a clo-- in barracks. They put over there, I don't know how long. I think-- I think we were three weeks over there. They were very nice people to us, but they don't let us to be free. And they told us until we don't get there to be free, we cannot go out. So it was, you know, until they make the papers and everything. And then after three weeks, we take a big bus, and we put a lot of children, and we came to Petah Tikva. Petah Tikva, this is a place. And we was over there a week in Petah Tikva. In Petah Tikva, came again our teacher, and ask me all the question. And he asked me if she want-- if I want to go to Jerusalem. I thought, I don't know what I want. I don't know. I am-- I am alone. But I am religious, so they took me to Jerusalem with-- with four girls. I have here a picture with two. And we came to Jerusalem, and it was a very nice [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Mizrahi. And we were so very sad. When we came, we get a very nice welcome. We don't know the language, nothing. And we was about 10 days together, the Hungarian people with the children. Later, they come, and they told us, we are going to separate. We cannot be over there together. So [NON-ENGLISH] over there, big institute, only girls. They take us, and they separate us. So I get out a room with five very nice girls. They speak other languages, and nobody speaks Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- this make with everybody. After three months, Zipora start to speak Hebrew perfect-- not perfect, but fluently. I can speak, I can understand. And when I saw the Hungarian girls, we speak only Hungarian. But it was the first three weeks, it was terrible. Because everybody start to speak, and we cannot understand nothing. So after a few weeks, we start-- we was together. And they make me examination, what kind of class can I go? And you know, I don't-- I almost finish, I don't know, four classes? Hungarian. So they told me that they have a low class. And I start, but I get very fast. And I start to learn Hebrew, and Tanakh, and [NON-ENGLISH]. And I jump. So I finish over there. And when did your family come to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only two years in Jerusalem because my motherr-- my mother arrived in Israel with my childre-- with my sisters in '48, exactly when it start the war. And we was in the war. And I-- we cannot leave Jerusalem and Tel Aviv. It was a problem. Because between Tel Aviv and Jerusalem, the people was over there fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know what? We don't get money, water. And the people from Jerusa-- from Tel Aviv-- in Tel Aviv was everything. In Jerusalem, not water, and not food, and they brought us everything. Again? When did--

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought-- and in '48, it was [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not ended. It was-- in '48 it was war. But it was a little-- it was a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: A cease fire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cease fire. And I, somebody come pick me up from Tel Aviv, and I take myself in night, in jeep. And I came to Tel Aviv to Netanya for my-- to my-- to my mother and to my sisters. They was in [NON-ENGLISH] in Netanya.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were together with them from then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come over there, they was in Netanya. And I was very happy to see them after two years again separate. And my-- and my teacher from [NON-ENGLISH] contact me and told me, Zipora, come back. I want you to continue to learn, because you stopped. Only two, two years is not enough for you. And you can be a [NON-ENGLISH] teaching a nursery-- teaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: A nurse or a teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: To be a teacher. And-- but I don't want to leave my mother. So I-- in '48 in September, we came and we get our-- a house in Jaffa-- Jaffa, Tel Aviv. And we was over there, and my brothers was in the army. And my mother gets sick. And my sister, Hannah, and I was the biggest, and my sister start again to go to work. And I start to work, and I stop to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, In '52 I meet him in Israel. I don't want to marry him. I was very young. And I don't know. I get married after three months, I don't know. But I am very, very, very happy. We have very happy marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was married in February 26 in '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name? What's your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betzalel. And we was married in Jaffa in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a very nice wedding, a very nice wedding. My husband makes the wedding. I don't have money. And he take me. I was very, very happy, and I am happy now, too. I have a very nice marriage. I have a very nice two nice girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are healthy, they are nice. I have a little very nice grandchild, Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your daughters' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Devorah and Brachah. Brachah is a little one, and Devorah is the biggest.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were born-- Devorah was born in eight-- month, April 18, '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: '54. And Brachah was in May 29, '58.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whom are they named for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brachah-- Devorah is after Betzalel's mother, and Brachah is after Betzalel's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in '79. In May-- in May-- no, in April, April '79.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and your sisters remained in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody left in Israel. What I want to tell you that my mother was with us 22 years in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had spoken at one time about the memorials, the annual memorials that they had in Israel-- the Yom HaShoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the celebrations that you had that you wrote a poem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I wrote, but I never spoke with nobody. I spoke only with my husband, with my family. But-- and everybody knows that I write in my family. And I write a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what is the celebration that they have in Israel every year?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have their Independence Day. They have their Independence Day. [INTERPOSING VOICES] They have the Independence Day. And before the Independence Day, have a special day, Yom HaShoah, they have. And they have the Yom HaShoah. This is a very special day for everybody. This is a remembering day. Everybody goes to the seminar, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cemeteries.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cemetary. And everybody going to-- to share with their families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is to remember the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is to remember. And I wrote this this time. I don't know if I can read, I try to read. But I know that I cry. Now-- I know that-- but I'll tell you something else. I want to tell you before I finish that my husband is dreaming every, every day, every night about the Holocaust. He's running, and he's all the time fighting with himself. And when my big daughter was born, when she was-- she came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Devorah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Devorah. It was a few months he was afraid to see her. She was a very nice girl. She was a very nice, cute girl. And he can not see him. He was afraid that something is very bad happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of what he had gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, and now I-- now I want to-- to read my story, my poem.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you write it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I write in April 17, '66, 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were living in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. And this is almost 30 years ago. And I want to read for you. This is a long poem.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am very sorry that you cannot understand, because-- but perhaps somebody going to understand. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think only he can understand. Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very special song that when you hear the-- [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sirens. Which sirens are these?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is for remembering. And everybody stand quiet about three minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. Everybody stop everything, and you stand, and you remember. And I saw again the-- the SS. And I saw the-- the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I saw again all the things, what-- what happening with all the peoples. And I saw again that they're-- they're alive, they're alive, but they are inside. They feel that they are-- have a lot of problem inside. And they do not-- they cannot forget-- never-- what is happening. This is a-- the song that I--

speaker: interviewer
text: That you wrote.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, that I wrote. This is not a song, this is a poem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now that you're looking back 50 years later, is there any last thing that you would like to say to people who'll be looking at the tape in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I want to tell you that I hope [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that what happening-- what happened, with me or with somebody else, or with with my husband, it never, never came. But you, I am afraid that it can happen again and again. I am afraid, because I don't know why the people-- why we are punished, or why the God punish us that we are like Job, and every time we are made to suffer. That's what I want to tell. And I leave all my life with remember. And I never want to be alone. And I'm afraid to be alone for five minutes even.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you have some pictures that you want to show us, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, if I-- yes, I want. This picture taked in Hungaria, in Hajdunanas. I am in the middle in the picture. And the other is my sisters. I think I was about three and a half or four here. And Esther. This picture was in Yugoslavia, I think about '42 or '43. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are my brother's wife and children. They-- they killed them one day in the yard. This picture was in Marseilles. with a lot of children before we-- we going to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the middle in the picture, downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the second row, sitting down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This picture took in Jerusalem with my-- my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am up in the-- I am stand in the second from right.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the top row?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was after you got to Israel in 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. This picture was making in Germany, and my mother send me to Israel to me. This is my sisters and brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us which is which?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is David. David is up. That's David, and Joseph, and Hannah. And the mother in the middle, and I have two sisters. One is sitting, Malka, and the other, standing up, is Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken before they came to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother name is Rachel. This is my wedding day with my. husband. It was in February 26, '59. '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jaffa.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. This in my two children, two daughters. Devorah is here, about seven. And Brachah is about three, three and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It taken in-- in my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- it was in Jaffa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the yard, outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the early 1960s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This picture took is me. We was-- came to visit Devorah, and we make our picture together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is my husband, Zipora-- myself, Brachah, Devorah, and I have over there my sister-in-law and her son, Ronnie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name, your sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Batya.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's married to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my husband's brother, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think '72. I don't remember exactly, '72, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1972, in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course in Israel. I was with my mother in a wedding day in a family place, and we was dancing together. I don't remember the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 1980s sometime?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: '80s, she's with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your mother pass away, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '87. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, '87.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Israel, this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Israel, yes. This picture is from Los Angeles. It is my two daughters. I think about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. They was walking, and we took the picture from there. This is Devorah and Brachah together. This is my grandchild. His name is Saul. This picture took in '93. He was in the nursery. He's a very happy, nice boy, and I love him very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Zipora, could you introduce us to people with you now, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sit near my husband. He's Betzalel Nutovits. We are married 43 years. We are very happy. And I hope that we're going to be a long time together. This is my daughter. Her name is Devorah. I lover her very much. She's a very nice girl, and she is living up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Upstairs from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Upstairs, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Devorah, you wanted to say something after your mother had finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I am-- especially for my father, since I was little, I remember my household, and my life was very influenced through his life, what happened. And he thought all the time that I don't know what's going on, and I never want to talk about it. And he thought he's not talking about the situation. But what I did-- I was very interested, and all the time I wanted to know what's going to go on. And he never really sit with me. And he was-- he was afraid to sit with me, that I will know what's going-- you know, because of my protection. But through the year, I gather stories by when he used to sit with my uncles, my cousins. And I-- when I took all the picture all together, and like this I knew what happened through his life. And until today, he thinks I don't know. But what happened-- I know everything what happened to him and to his brothers. But what I want to tell-- my life is very-- in a way, we have it, we have it and we feel it at home, whatever he passed through. More with him than with my mother. My mother put it inside. But with my father, it's very strongly affected. And it's got to me too-- to me and my little sister in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you, and thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
